data.title: A 20-Year-Old Was 'Guilt-Tripped' By Her Mom To Co-Sign One Car And Secretly Signed Her Onto Another. 'Both Cars Have Been Repossessed'
data.description:
A strong credit score can take years to build and months to destroy. For one young woman, the damage started at 20 years old, when she says her mother “guilt-tripped” her into co-signing a car loan.

By 26, she was staring at roughly $20,000 in debt tied to two repossessed vehicles and trying to figure out how to move forward with her new husband.

A Costly Family Decision

The caller, Arly, shared her story on “The Ramsey Show” with hosts Jade Warshaw and George Kamel. She said that at 20 or 21, she had an 800 credit score. Then her mom pressured her to co-sign for a car.

“She guilt-tripped me into doing all of that,” Arly said. 

Don't Miss:

When Kamel pushed back and told her she was already a grown woman at the time, she responded that there was a cultural pressure at play. “Like ‘I’ve done so much for you. I need you to do this for me,'” Kamel interjected. “And you just went, ‘Okay, fine.'” Arly said that’s exactly what happened.

“Both cars have been repossessed,” she said.

After the vehicles were auctioned off, she was left with deficiency balances of about $10,000 on one car and $8,000 on the other. On top of that, she took out a $2,000 personal loan at her mother's urging.

Trending: Designed for investors with strong market convictions, REX Shares builds ETFs for income, leverage, and tactical positioning — explore the lineup.

Altogether, around $20,000 in debt was tied to decisions she made in her early 20s.

Warshaw acknowledged the complicated dynamic. “There is an imbalance of power there when you’re 18, 21 years old and a parent is saying you need to do this,” she said. 

Still, Kamel made it clear that creditors do not care about the backstory. “You just need to act like this is debt I took on and I got to clean the mess up,” he said.

The hosts advised Arly to attempt to settle the repossession balances for 40% to 50% of what is owed, using lump-sum payments. “Anytime you’re going to settle a debt, you’ve got to have the cash in hand ready,” Warshaw said, stressing the importance of getting agreements in writing.

They also urged her to freeze her credit and pull all three credit reports to make sure no additional accounts had been opened in her name.

Marriage, Money And Moving Forward

Arly recently got married and said she hesitated to involve her husband in fixing the problem. “I don’t want him to take on the burden,” she said.

Warshaw pushed back gently. “If the tables were turned, would you be willing to help him?” she asked.

“Of course,” Arly replied.

“As long as you’re not asking more of someone than you would want asked of yourself, then I think it’s okay,” Warshaw said, adding that marriage means tackling problems as a team.

Beyond the immediate debt, the deeper lesson was about boundaries. “No more debt, no more co-signing,” Warshaw said. “Never again is really the line in the sand that you need to draw.”

For households earning $100,000 or more, financial missteps like this can still result in years of cleanup. Getting objective guidance can help couples align on strategy and avoid repeating painful patterns. WiserAdvisor offers a free matching tool that connects you with a vetted financial advisor who fits your needs, with no obligation to hire. 

Arly's situation isn’t uncommon. Family pressure, cultural expectations and emotional guilt can result in costly decisions. But as the hosts emphasized, the path forward starts with ownership, teamwork and firm financial boundaries.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Dogecoin Vs Shiba Inu: Both Testing Support—Which Breaks Out First?
data.description:
Dogecoin (CRYPTO: DOGE) and Shiba Inu (CRYPTO: SHIB) are trading flat, with both meme coins compressed in symmetrical triangles signaling imminent 10-15% moves in either direction.

Dogecoin’s Coiled Spring

DOGE’s Parabolic SAR flipped below price at $0.09562—the first constructive signal in weeks suggesting the $0.083 capitulation low may have marked bottom. 

However, the Supertrend at $0.10378 remains bearish, sandwiching DOGE between conflicting indicators.

An ascending trendline from $0.083 provides support while a descending trendline from January highs caps rallies around $0.115-$0.12, creating a wedge compressing price between $0.095-$0.105.

Derivatives data shows building conviction. Open interest jumped 2.30% to $1.08 billion while volume surged 16.66% to $2.10 billion—the kind of surge that precedes explosive moves. 

Long/short ratios show heavy bullish positioning: Binance at 2.6483, OKX at 3.54. This creates liquidation fuel if DOGE breaks down but also short-squeeze potential if it breaks higher.

Critical levels: Breaking above the Supertrend at $0.1038 opens $0.105-$0.107, then $0.11-$0.115. Breaking below $0.095 triggers liquidation cascades toward $0.090-$0.093 or retesting $0.083.

Shiba Inu’s Triangle Compression

SHIB grinds sideways with all EMAs tightly clustered: 20 EMA at $0.00000635, 50 EMA at $0.00000638, 100 EMA at $0.00000648, and 200 EMA at $0.00000683. 

This compression after months of decline signals downtrend exhaustion before the next directional move.

A symmetrical triangle is forming between an ascending support from the $0.00000515 capitulation low and descending resistance, with the apex at $0.00000640-$0.00000650.

Breakouts from these patterns typically deliver 10-15% moves.

Token burns surged 1,900% in 24 hours with 1 million SHIB burned. Recent large burns include multiple 1 million SHIB transactions and a massive 2.4 million burn five days ago.

This reduces circulating supply and provides fundamental support, though 585.47 trillion SHIB still circulates.

The Supertrend at $0.00000653 sits just overhead in bearish mode. 

Breaking above flips momentum bullish and targets $0.0000068-$0.000007 where the 200 EMA resides. 

Breaking below $0.00000625 invalidates the triangle and triggers selling toward $0.000006 or $0.0000055-$0.0000057.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Trump Doubles Down On Tariffs After Supreme Court Blow: The Week On Wall Street
data.description:
It was shaping up to be a relatively quiet week for markets, until Friday delivered a cascade of market-moving headlines.

Fresh economic data showed the U.S. economy expanded at just a 1.4% annualized pace in the fourth quarter, a sharp slowdown from 4.4% in the prior quarter and well below expectations for 3% growth.

• State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust stock is trading near recent highs. What’s the outlook for SPY shares?

At the same time, the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge accelerated to a 3% annual rate in December, with the monthly increase of 0.4% marking the fastest pace since February.

President Donald Trump pointed to the prolonged government shutdown as a key driver of the weak growth print, arguing it shaved at least two percentage points off GDP.

But the real jolt came hours later, when the Supreme Court issued a landmark decision striking down the administration's tariff authority under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA).

The Court ruled that IEEPA does not authorize the president to impose tariffs, grounding its reasoning in separation of powers. "

“The Framers gave ‘Congress alone' the power to impose tariffs during peacetime," the Court wrote, adding "the whole power of taxation rests with Congress."

The Court did not address whether the administration must refund more than $130 billion in tariffs already collected under the emergency declarations.

"The good news is that there are methods, practices, statutes and authorities… that are even stronger than the IEEPA tariffs available to me," Trump said in a press conference following the decision.

In response to Court’s ruling, Trump unveiled a new 10% global tariff under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 and confirmed that existing Section 232 and Section 301 tariffs remain "fully in place and in full force and effect."

He said, "We have alternatives. Great alternatives. I can charge much more than I was charging," he added.

Trump suggested the refund issue could face years of litigation.

A day earlier, trade data revealed another blow for the America First agenda.

The U.S. goods and services deficit widened to $70 billion in December, while the full-year 2025 trade gap reached $901 billion — essentially unchanged from 2024 — suggesting tariffs have had no impact on narrowing the imbalance, a central goal of the administration's policy.

Meanwhile, economists and policymakers are increasingly focused on artificial intelligence's growing impact on the labor market.

Federal Reserve Governor Michael Barr recently flagged mounting challenges for younger workers in digital services.

In an exclusive interview with Benzinga, Oxford Economics economist Nico Palesch warned that advances in robotics could soon disrupt physical jobs, potentially affecting up to 20% of the workforce.

data.title: Blue Hat Interactive Stock Craters After Dilutive $6.4M Offering
data.description:
• Blue Hat Interactive shares are approaching critical lows. Why are BHAT shares at support?

Aggressive Warrant Structure Heightens Dilution Risk

The company is selling 32 million units at 20 cents, each made up of one ordinary share and one warrant to buy another share at the same price.

The warrants are immediately exercisable for one year and can reset the exercise price to 70% and then 50% of the initial level on the second and fifth trading days after closing, increasing the underlying share count.

Holders may also opt for a cashless, zero-exercise feature that delivers twice the number of shares versus a standard cash exercise. The deal includes a 15-day option for the underwriter to buy an extra 4.8 million units, potentially deepening dilution.

Proceeds Fund Pivot Toward Commodity Trading

Blue Hat expects the offering to close Feb. 23, with proceeds earmarked for working capital and general corporate purposes as it shifts away from its legacy augmented-reality games business toward global commodity trading.

BHAT Shares Drop Over 70% Friday

BHAT Price Action: Blue Hat Interactive shares were down 75.17% at $0.11 at the time of publication on Friday. The stock is trading at a new 52-week low, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Easy Income Portfolio: February 2026 Edition
data.description:
Credit Markets, Income Assets, and the Software Panic That Wasn't

Markets rarely announce risk in obvious ways. The past month is a perfect example. Major indexes have appeared calm on the surface, but beneath that calm, the underlying machinery continues to shift.

Credit spreads remain historically tight, signaling continued confidence in borrower health and funding conditions. At the same time, rising interest rate volatility, reflected in an uptick in the MOVE index, suggests investors are becoming more cautious. This combination—tight spreads alongside growing rate volatility—often leads to sentiment-driven overreactions, particularly in income assets where investors tend to assume the worst quickly.

Easy Income has always been built around durable cash flow rather than market narratives. This month reinforced why that approach works. Across private credit, energy income, mortgage-backed securities, sovereign debt, and preferred stocks, income streams remain intact even as sentiment fluctuates.

The numbers matter more than the headlines. And the numbers remain supportive.

Private Credit and BDCs: The Software Panic Is Sentiment, Not Substance

Private credit has once again found itself cast as the villain of the moment. This time, the concern revolves around artificial intelligence and its perceived threat to software borrowers.

The market reaction has been swift. Business development companies with exposure to software lending sold off sharply, driven more by fear than by actual credit deterioration.

This reaction ignores a fundamental reality. Software lending has been a cornerstone of private credit for over a decade precisely because these companies generate recurring revenue, high margins, and predictable cash flow. Enterprise software does not disappear overnight. Contracts are long-term. Customer relationships are sticky. Mission-critical infrastructure does not get replaced impulsively.

More importantly, private credit structures provide significant protection. These loans typically sit senior in the capital structure, supported by covenants, sponsor equity, and negotiated flexibility. Even when operating conditions weaken, lenders can restructure terms, adjust pricing, or rely on private equity sponsors to protect enterprise value.

This is not venture capital. It is structured lending backed by real cash flow.

When diversified BDC portfolios trade at discounts to net asset value because of hypothetical disruption rather than realized credit impairment, history suggests income investors are being handed opportunity—not risk.

Ironically, artificial intelligence could strengthen many borrowers by improving efficiency and margins. The selloff reflects crowd psychology, not structural deterioration.

Energy Income: Cash Flow Remains Stable Despite Commodity Noise

Energy income continues to benefit from a balanced commodity environment. Oil prices remain high enough to support steady production and cash flow, but not so elevated that they trigger reckless capital expansion.

Royalty trusts and production-linked income vehicles are designed to reflect commodity volatility. Their distributions fluctuate naturally with production and pricing cycles. That variability is not a weakness. It is how the structure converts real asset output into income.

Midstream infrastructure remains particularly attractive. Pipelines and processing assets function more like toll roads than speculative exploration businesses. Cash flow is driven by transport volumes rather than commodity prices directly.

Long-term demand trends remain favorable, driven by global LNG exports, rising electricity consumption, and infrastructure expansion tied to data center growth.

These are structural demand drivers, not temporary cycles.

Mortgage-Backed Securities: Stability Is Returning

Agency mortgage-backed securities have quietly improved as mortgage spreads stabilized and rate volatility moderated.

These assets carry minimal credit risk, backed by government-sponsored entities. The primary risk is interest rate volatility, not borrower default.

Periods of elevated rate volatility often create temporary dislocations, pushing mortgage securities to attractive yield levels despite stable fundamentals. That dynamic creates opportunities for disciplined accumulation.

Mortgage income remains one of the most structurally secure income streams available.

Commercial Mortgage Credit: Structure Matters More Than Headlines

Commercial real estate continues to generate concern, but the reality is far more nuanced.

Senior commercial mortgage tranches remain well protected, supported by conservative underwriting and structural priority in the capital stack. Lower-rated tranches remain more vulnerable, particularly in weaker office markets.

Commercial mortgage credit is not a uniform risk. The structure determines the outcome.

Income investors focused on senior tranches continue to benefit from strong structural protection and attractive yields.

Corporate Bonds: Late-Cycle Conditions Require Discipline

Corporate credit spreads remain tight, reflecting continued investor demand for yield and confidence in borrower solvency.

This environment supports income generation but also requires discipline. Tight spreads leave less room for error and increase sensitivity to sentiment shifts.

This is a classic late-cycle setup. Credit markets remain open and functional, but selective positioning becomes increasingly important.

Easy Income remains focused on structure, diversification, and patience.

Closed-End Funds and Activism: Hidden Value Beyond Yield

Activism has reemerged as a powerful catalyst in the closed-end fund universe.

Persistent discounts to net asset value are attracting activist investors pushing for tender offers, restructurings, and governance changes designed to unlock value.

For income investors, these situations offer a dual benefit:

  • Ongoing income from distributions
  • Potential upside from discount narrowing

This creates an embedded margin of safety independent of market timing.

Preferred Stocks: Quietly Offering Exceptional Risk-Adjusted Income

Preferred securities continue to trade below par across many high-quality issuers.

This provides income investors with elevated yields and potential price appreciation if spreads normalize.

Preferred stocks occupy a unique position between debt and equity, offering income priority while retaining upside potential.

This remains one of the most attractive segments of the income market.

Portfolio Review: Core Easy Income Holdings

The Easy Income portfolio remains diversified across income sectors, combining private credit, preferred stocks, real assets, sovereign debt, and structured credit.

Core positions include:

Each position plays a specific role in balancing income generation, structural protection, and diversification.

The Bottom Line: Sentiment Is Moving Faster Than Fundamentals

The dominant theme this month has been overreaction, not deterioration.

Credit markets remain healthy. Income streams remain intact. Structural protections remain strong.

What has changed is sentiment.

Markets will always oscillate between fear and complacency. Easy Income exists to take advantage of those swings by focusing on what actually drives long-term results: durable cash flow, disciplined structure, and patient capital allocation.

Income investing is not about predicting headlines. It is about collecting cash flow while others react to narratives.

And right now, the income continues to arrive exactly as expected.
